title: The clash over AI doctors has begun, and its front line is in Utah. It ...
full_text:
The Wall Street Journal's Post
The Wall Street Journal
9h ·
The clash over AI doctors has begun, and its front line is in Utah.
It started with technology from the startup Doctronic. The state launched a project in January that will allow the service to renew prescriptions for patients. The hope is it will improve medication access.
Any Utah adult can log in to get a refill on cholesterol medications or antidepressants, among other prescriptions. When fully implemented, the project will break new ground by letting an artificial-intelligence product, acting on its own, perform this job typically done by physicians.
Doctors aren’t happy about it.
“People can have life and death reactions to medications,” said Dr. Alan Smith, a family physician who chairs the Utah medical board but said he wasn’t speaking for the group. “And then I worry about liability. Who is actually liable for problems that may occur because of a refill of a medication?”
Read more: https://on.wsj.com/4oypzbf
All reactions:
66
38 comments
25 shares
Like
Comment
Most relevant
Gregory Bradshaw The creators of doctronic would be liable.
3h
View 1 reply
Brett Dupree You're right, your records do say you have a deadly allergy.
30m
Mel Viajante Of course it’s Utah.
1h
Bidyut Mukhopadhyay AI doctors can make a huge difference in healthcare, especially in improving medication access, and I think the benefits will be seen in both rural and urban areas, improving health outcomes overall.
5h
Nick Bonomo Manic switching from antidepressants comes out at about 3-8% in patients with no known prior history of mania. These reactions are fairly common in psychiatry but can be life ending. AI is most likely not ready to detect these changes and this initiative will result in some individuals entering mixed mood episodes and taking their life.
Us physicians really just want patients to heal and have happy successful lives. Please try to listen to us when we say that these kinds of changes to our system are dangerous and science would call for better oversight and research before this kind of initiative is approved.
9h
13
View all 2 replies
Patrick Donovan the only clash that is happening is pointless rich execs wanting to push MDs out so they can make the medical decisions. only problem is AI no where near able to make medical calls, it's a glorified chat bot
1h
Teresa Manuszak I usually hate AI. But our arrogant doctors have screwed up so many times I'd consider this.
4h
Evan Fraenkel Oh no, their billable hours
